params.text:
Reducing the risk of falls

Because falls are multifactorial, prevention works best when several small changes are made together. Much of it is about the environment and simple practical support tailored to the individual.

  • Keep the environment tidy, well lit and free of trip hazards such as loose rugs and clutter
  • Make sure walking aids are the right height, in good repair and kept within reach
  • Encourage well-fitting, supportive footwear rather than loose slippers
  • Keep everyday items – call bell, drink, glasses, phone – within easy reach
  • Support prompt access to the toilet, especially at night
  • Follow the person's care plan and any falls risk assessment

params.question: Which set of actions best helps prevent falls for a person you support at home?

answers.text: Clear trip hazards, keep the call bell and glasses within reach, and check their walking frame is in good order

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Addressing the environment, keeping essentials reachable and checking aids all reduce risk together.

answers.text: Encourage them to hurry to the toilet so they are not waiting

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Rushing, especially at night, is a common cause of falls. Support prompt but unhurried access instead.

answers.text: Move their walking frame out of the way to free up space

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Walking aids must be kept within easy reach, not tidied away, so the person can use them safely.

answers.text: Leave loose rugs in place as they make the room feel homely

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Loose rugs are a classic trip hazard and should be secured or removed even if familiar.
